title: Training SentencePiece
concept: Train SentencePiece directly on raw text files; it outputs a model file and a vocab file ready for embedding lookup layers.

exercises.prompt: What is the main purpose of SentencePiece in natural language processing?
exercises.explain: SentencePiece is a subword tokenizer that helps convert raw text into smaller pieces (tokens) that an AI model can easily process.

cards.title: How AI Learns to Read
cards.body: Before an AI can read human books, it needs a vocabulary. But who decides what units of words go into this vocabulary? Instead of doing it by hand, we train SentencePiece directly on raw text to figure it out for us!

cards.title: What is SentencePiece Training?
cards.body: Training SentencePiece means running a program directly on raw text files. It automatically analyzes the text and spits out two key items: a `.model` file (which knows how to chop new text) and a `.vocab` file (a dictionary of subwords with their frequency ranks).

cards.title: The Subword Chef
cards.body: Imagine you are giving a recipe book and ingredients to a smart chef. SentencePiece is that chef! You give it a massive text document (the ingredients), and it automatically slices it into the most useful parts (the words and subwords) to create a custom menu (the vocabulary file).

cards.title: Training in Action
cards.body:
To train SentencePiece in Python, you simply run:
`spm.SentencePieceTrainer.train('--input=book.txt --model_prefix=my_model --vocab_size=8000')`

This reads your text file `book.txt` and automatically generates `my_model.model` and `my_model.vocab` on your computer.

cards.title: Do We Need to Pre-split?
cards.body:
Myth: You need to clean, lowercase, and pre-split your text into words using spaces before training SentencePiece.
Reality: SentencePiece is designed to train directly on raw, messy text files! It treats spaces just like any other character, making it perfect for languages like Chinese or Japanese that don't use spaces.

cards.title: Training Pipeline Comparison
cards.body: Here is how SentencePiece simplifies the pipeline compared to older tokenizers.

left.text: Goes directly into SentencePiece training, no pre-tokenization needed.

right.text: Required manual rules to split text by spaces and punctuation first.

exercises.prompt: Which two files are output by the SentencePiece training process?

options.text: A model file (.model) and a vocabulary file (.vocab)
A Python script and a text file containing only spaces
An HTML web page and a database folder
A spreadsheet file and a list of punctuation marks

exercises.explain: The model file (.model) is used to actually split new text into tokens, while the vocabulary file (.vocab) lists all the subwords that have been learned.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The trained model file (.model) is used to split new text into subwords for embedding lookup layers.
exercises.explain: The model file contains the logic needed to split new, unseen text into tokens during inference.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe what kind of files SentencePiece trains on:
exercises.sentence: SentencePiece is trained directly on raw text files without needing pre-tokenization.
exercises.explain: SentencePiece processes raw text without requiring any pre-tokenization, generating the vocabulary and model directly from the raw files.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes SentencePiece's training input requirements?

options.text: SentencePiece requires text to be pre-tokenized into space-separated words.
SentencePiece can train directly on raw, un-tokenized text files.
SentencePiece requires all capital letters to be deleted first.

exercises.explain: SentencePiece trains directly on raw, un-tokenized text documents, meaning we do not need to split words with spaces beforehand.

exercises.prompt: A junior developer asks you what the exact inputs and outputs of training SentencePiece are. What is your response?

options.text: Raw text files go in; a .model file and a .vocab file come out.
Pre-tokenized words go in; python source code comes out.
Images go in; a list of pixel values comes out.
Python dictionaries go in; a trained neural network weights file comes out.

exercises.explain: SentencePiece is designed to read raw text files and automatically output a binary model file (.model) along with a human-readable vocabulary list (.vocab) containing the learned subwords and their scores.
